name: Archivo
classification_notes: Classified as sans-serif on the page; described as a grotesque font reminiscent of late nineteenth century American typefaces, placing it firmly in the grotesque subcategory.
summary: A grotesque sans by Hector Gatti, originally conceived for headlines and highlights, drawing on late nineteenth century American type traditions.
description: Designed by Hector Gatti, Archivo takes its cues from the robust grotesques of late nineteenth century America, making it well-suited to display and headline use. The family spans four weights from Regular to Bold, each with a matching italic, giving it enough range for editorial hierarchies. Released under the SIL Open Font Licence, it is freely available for use and modification.
foundry_blurb: Archivo is a grotesque font by Hector Gatti, originally designed for highlights and headlines. The family is described as reminiscent of late nineteenth century American typefaces and is available under the SIL Open Font License v.1.1.

name: Aileron
classification_notes: Described as having a geometric structure with clean, modern lines; the foundry classifies it as sans-serif, consistent with a geometric grotesque approach.
summary: A geometric sans-serif by Sori Sagano released under CC0, offering eight weights with matching italics for editorial and interface work.
description: A clean geometric sans with generous spacing and a light, airy character, spanning eight weights from Ultralight to Black, each with an italic companion. The CC0 licence places it fully in the public domain, making it freely usable without restriction. Suited to minimalist branding, editorial layouts and interface design where legibility and restraint are priorities.
foundry_blurb: Open Foundry describes Aileron as a clean, modern sans-serif with a light, airy feel. Its geometric structure and generous spacing give it a refined, contemporary character, and the foundry positions it as ideal for minimalist branding, editorial design and interfaces that value clarity and elegance. The specimen was created by Andreas Faust.

name: M+ M Type-1
classification_notes: Classified as sans-serif on the page; the mix of smooth curves and straight lines with broad language support including Japanese kanji points to a humanist sans sensibility rather than a purely geometric or grotesque approach.
summary: A Japanese-Latin sans-serif by Coji Morishita covering over 5,700 kanji and the GF Latin Plus character set across five weights.
description: Designed by Coji Morishita, M+ M Type-1 combines smooth curves with straight lines to produce a clean, open appearance suited to both text and display use. Five weights from Thin to Bold are available, and the family supports a broad range of Japanese kanji alongside extended Latin coverage. Released under the SIL Open Font Licence, making it freely available for use and modification.
foundry_blurb: Open Foundry describe M+ M Type-1 as a sans-serif by Coji Morishita offering nine weights from Thin to Black, covering the GF Latin Plus set and more than 5,700 Japanese kanji. They note that its combination of smooth curves and straight lines gives a modern, open impression suited to everything from small text to large titles.

name: Bluu Next
classification_notes: Classified as serif by the foundry. The sharp, precise character and positioning as a replacement for staid serifs like Times suggests a display-oriented serif with strong contrast.
summary: A sharp, high-contrast serif by Jean-Baptiste Morizot, available in Bold and Bold Italic under the SIL Open Font Licence.
description: Designed by Jean-Baptiste Morizot, this is a bold, precise serif intended as a livelier alternative to workaday faces like Times New Roman. The family is deliberately minimal, offering Bold and Bold Italic only, which positions it firmly as a display or headline choice rather than a body text workhorse. Released under the SIL Open Font Licence, making it freely available for use and modification.
foundry_blurb: Open Foundry describes Bluu Next Bold as sharp and precise, and positions it as a totally good replacement for boring serif fonts like Times. The specimen was created by Jon Bland.

name: Work Sans
classification_notes: Classified as sans-serif by the foundry; inspired by early Grotesques with simplified features optimised for digital use, placing it squarely in the grotesque subcategory.
summary: A nine-weight grotesque by Wei Huang, optimised for on-screen reading with simplified details and enlarged diacritics.
description: Drawn by Wei Huang and inspired by early grotesque typefaces, Work Sans adapts its forms for digital environments through simplified details and larger diacritic marks. The nine weights run from Thin to Black, with the core middle weights suited to body text and the extremes intended for display use. All weights include matching italics, giving the family broad range across UI and editorial applications.
foundry_blurb: Work Sans is a nine-weight typeface family by Wei Huang, inspired by early Grotesques. The core weights are designed for on-screen text while the lightest and heaviest are suited for display. Features are simplified for digital use, with larger diacritic marks. Released under the SIL Open Font License v1.1.

name: Tex Gyre Heros
classification_notes: Based on URW Nimbus Sans, itself derived from Helvetica, placing it firmly in the neo-grotesque tradition of rational, closed-aperture sans-serifs.
summary: An open-source neo-grotesque based on URW Nimbus Sans, intended as a free-licensed substitute for Helvetica.
description: Built atop URW Nimbus Sans L, itself a close derivative of Helvetica, this family offers eight styles spanning regular and bold weights in upright, italic and condensed cuts. Vietnamese support was contributed by Han The Thanh, broadening its language coverage beyond standard Latin. Released under the GUST Font Licence, it functions as a practical open-source stand-in where Helvetica metrics or aesthetics are needed.
foundry_blurb: TeX Gyre Heros is based on URW Nimbus Sans L, released by URW++ Design and Development Inc under the GFL. Vietnamese glyphs were added by Han The Thanh. It can serve as a replacement for Helvetica, also known as Swiss, designed by Max Miedinger with Eduard Hoffmann in 1957 at the Haas Type Foundry.

name: Reglo
classification_notes: Classified as sans-serif on the page; the geometric construction is confirmed by the foundry's own description and its origin in a radio station identity project.
summary: A single-weight geometric sans drawn by Sebastien Sanfilippo for the Radio Panik visual identity, released under an open licence.
description: Originally created for Radio Panik in collaboration with Open Source Publishing, this geometric sans is available in a single Bold weight at 700. The specimen was produced by Anthony Burrill, lending the release a distinct graphic character. Distributed under the SIL Open Font License, making it freely usable and modifiable.
foundry_blurb: Reglo Bold is described by Open Foundry as a geometric sans designed by Sebastien Sanfilippo, originally created for the Radio Panik identity in collaboration with Open Source Publishing, with a specimen produced by Anthony Burrill.

name: Roboto
classification_notes: Roboto is classified as a sans-serif with humanist qualities, blending geometric structure with open letterforms and natural rhythm suited to screen environments.
summary: Google's signature sans-serif, designed by Christian Robertson as the default typeface for Android and Chrome OS across eighteen styles.
description: Designed by Christian Robertson for Google, Roboto serves as the default typeface on Android and Chrome OS and the recommended font for the Material Design visual language. The family spans nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, covering Latin, Cyrillic and Greek characters in Unicode 7.0 alongside the Georgian lari currency symbol. Its mix of geometric structure and open, humanist letterforms makes it a reliable workhorse for on-screen text at most sizes.
foundry_blurb: Open Foundry hosts Roboto as an SIL Open Font License release. The family was created by Christian Robertson as Google's signature font, used as the default typeface on Android and Chrome OS and the recommended font for Material Design. It supports all Latin, Cyrillic and Greek characters in Unicode 7.0 and is available in eighteen styles.

name: Terminal Grotesque
classification_notes: The foundry classifies it as display; its origins in a pixel icon font and influence from Futura and Radim Pesko's grotesque drawings place it firmly in geometric display territory, with pixel-grid shaping giving it a distinctive constructed quality.
summary: A pixel-grid-influenced display grotesque by Raphaël Bastide and Jérémy Landes, grown from a Fontstruct experiment into an open-source title face.
description: Originally developed in 2010 as a follow-up to Modulicon, a pixel icon font, Terminal Grotesque evolved through Fontstruct and Fontforge with Futura and Radim Pesko's grotesque drawings as reference points. The pixel grid left a visible mark on its construction, giving it a mechanical, structured quality suited to large-scale title use. Released under the SIL Open Font Licence, it continues to develop through community contributions.
foundry_blurb: Open Foundry describe Terminal Grotesque as a display typeface that began in 2010 as a follow-up to Modulicon, a pixel icon font, growing into a larger title face influenced by Futura and Radim Pesko's grotesque drawings. First built in Fontstruct and later refined in Fontforge, it was shaped by constant revisions on the pixel grid. It is an open-source project, free to use and adapt, and continues to evolve through contributions from its designers and the wider community.

name: Oswald
classification_notes: Oswald is explicitly described as a reworking of the classic gothic style, referencing Alternate Gothic as a direct antecedent. Its condensed, upright forms with redrawn characters place it firmly in the gothic condensed sans-serif tradition.
summary: A screen-optimised reworking of the classic gothic condensed style, redrawing characters to sit cleanly on the pixel grid.
description: Drawn by Vernon Adams with contributions from Cyreal and Kalapi Gajjar, Oswald takes the condensed gothic tradition exemplified by Alternate Gothic and adapts it for on-screen use. Characters have been redrawn to align with the pixel grid, making it practical across desktops, laptops and mobile devices. Six weights from Extralight to Bold are available under the SIL Open Font Licence.
foundry_blurb: Open Foundry describe Oswald as a reworking of the classic gothic style, citing Alternate Gothic as a reference point. The characters have been redrawn to fit the pixel grid of modern screens, making it suitable for free use across the internet on desktops, laptops and mobile devices.

name: Poppins
classification_notes: Poppins is a geometric sans-serif with near-circular letterforms, consistent stroke widths and a clean, modular construction typical of the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A geometric sans-serif with nine weights and matching italics, designed by Jonny Pinhorn for ITF and released under the SIL Open Font License.
description: Each letterform in Poppins is grounded in near-perfect circular geometry, giving the family a clean and consistent rhythm across its eighteen styles. The range runs from Thin to Black with matching italics at every weight, making it a practical choice for interfaces, editorial work and display use. Its open licence and broad weight range have made it a widely adopted choice in web and product design.
foundry_blurb: Open Foundry lists Poppins as a sans-serif typeface created by Jonny Pinhorn and ITF, available under the SIL Open Font License v.1.1, with a specimen designed by Matthias Wagner.

name: Ostrich Sans
classification_notes: Classified as display by the foundry; the typeface's defining characteristic is an unusually elongated neck giving letters extreme vertical extension, making it suited to headline and display use rather than text setting.
summary: A display sans with unusually elongated letterforms across five weights, released under an open licence by Tyler Finck.
description: Designed by Tyler Finck, Ostrich Sans is built around a strikingly tall, extended neck that gives each letterform an elongated, almost architectural quality. Five weights run from Light (300) through to Heavy (900), covering a broad range of display applications from editorial headlines to poster work. Available under the SIL Open Font Licence, it is freely usable and redistributable.
foundry_blurb: Open Foundry describe Ostrich Sans as a modern sans serif with an extended neck, available in a wide range of styles and weights, designed by Tyler Finck and licensed under the SIL Open Font Licence v1.1.

name: Office Code Pro
classification_notes: Explicitly classified as monospace on the page; a customised derivative of Source Code Pro, a monospaced sans-serif designed for coding environments.
summary: A customised derivative of Source Code Pro, adjusted by Nathan Rutzky specifically for text editors and coding environments.
description: Based on Paul D. Hunt's Source Code Pro for Adobe Systems, Nathan Rutzky's modifications refine the face for coding contexts while remaining practical in other settings. The family covers four weights from Light to Bold, each with a matching italic, totalling eight styles. Released under the SIL Open Font Licence.
foundry_blurb: Office Code Pro is a customised version of Source Code Pro, the monospaced sans serif created by Paul D. Hunt for Adobe Systems. Adjustments were made with text editors and coding environments in mind, though the face works well in other settings too.

name: Young Serif
classification_notes: The foundry classifies it as serif; inspired by old style designs such as Plantin Infant and ITC Italian Old Style, with humanist proportions and rounded details characteristic of the old style genre.
summary: A variable old style serif with rounded details, drawn in the tradition of Plantin Infant and ITC Italian Old Style by Bastien Sozeau.
description: Rounded terminals on characters such as the lowercase b and f give this old style serif a soft, generous quality that distinguishes it from more austere historical revivals. Designed by Bastien Sozeau and first distributed by the studio Uplaod, it is offered as a single variable Regular weight. Released under the SIL Open Font License, it is free to use, modify and redistribute.
foundry_blurb: Open Foundry describes Young Serif as a variable old style serif inspired by Plantin Infant and ITC Italian Old Style, noting its rounded details as the source of its soft and generous character. Designed by Bastien Sozeau with specimen work by Charlotte Coulais, it was first distributed by studio Uplaod and is open source under the SIL Open Font License v1.1.

name: Nimbus Sans L
classification_notes: A grotesque sans-serif derived from Helvetica sources, with metrics closely matching both Helvetica and Arial, placing it firmly in the neo-grotesque tradition.
summary: A 1987 GPL-licensed grotesque derived from Helvetica sources, with near-identical metrics to both Helvetica and Arial.
description: Released in 1987 by URW Type Foundry, Nimbus Sans L was produced from Adobe sources as a metrically compatible alternative to Helvetica and Arial. A subset was later released under the GNU General Public License v3.0, making it one of the earlier open-source grotesque options available. The family covers four styles: Regular, Italic, Bold and Bold Italic.
foundry_blurb: Open Foundry describes Nimbus Sans L as a 1987 version of Nimbus Sans based on Adobe sources. A subset was released under the GPL, and while its characters are not identical, its metrics are almost the same as Helvetica and Arial.

name: Liberation Sans
classification_notes: Liberation Sans is a metric-compatible substitute for Arial, itself a grotesque sans-serif, sharing its proportions and spacing to ensure identical text flow across documents.
summary: A freely licensed grotesque designed for full metric compatibility with Arial, preserving text flow and layout across systems and documents.
description: Designed by Steve Matteson, Liberation Sans replicates the exact metrics of Arial so that documents typeset in one can be exchanged for the other without any reflow. The family covers four styles: Regular, Italic, Bold and Bold Italic. Released under the SIL Open Font License, it is a practical substitute for environments where Arial is unavailable or proprietary fonts are restricted.
foundry_blurb: Liberation Sans is described as a typeface designed for full metric compatibility with Arial, Times New Roman and Courier New, offering a freely available substitute that preserves identical text flow and layout across documents and systems.

name: League Gothic
designer: Tyler Finck, Caroline Hadilaksono, Micah Rich
classification_notes: A condensed grotesque sans-serif, reviving Morris Fuller Benton's Alternate Gothic No 1 from 1903, characterised by extreme vertical compression and narrow proportions typical of American display grotesques.
summary: A revival of Morris Fuller Benton's Alternate Gothic No 1 from 1903, redrawn as a free, open-source condensed grotesque for display use.
description: Originally designed by Morris Fuller Benton for American Type Founders in 1903, Alternate Gothic No 1 entered the public domain following ATF's closure in 1993. This revival by Tyler Finck, Caroline Hadilaksono and Micah Rich brings the design into a freely licensed family covering Regular, Condensed, Italic and Condensed Italic cuts. Its extreme vertical compression makes it well suited to headlines, posters and display settings where strong typographic presence is needed in a narrow column.
foundry_blurb: Open Foundry describe League Gothic as a revival of the classic Alternate Gothic No 1, originally designed by Morris Fuller Benton for American Type Founders in 1903. Following ATF's closure in 1993 and with the design predating 1923, the typeface entered the public domain and lives on today in this modern, widely used reinterpretation, released under the SIL Open Font License v.1.1.

name: Libre Baskerville
classification_notes: Based on the American Type Founders' Baskerville of 1941, a canonical transitional serif; modifications to x-height, counters and contrast are screen-oriented adaptations rather than stylistic departures from the underlying classification.
summary: A web-optimised revival of the 1941 ATF Baskerville, redrawn with a taller x-height and wider counters for screen legibility at body sizes.
description: Designed by Pablo Impallari and Rodrigo Fuenzalida, the family departs from its ATF source by increasing the x-height, opening the counters and reducing stroke contrast, all in service of on-screen readability at around 16px. Three styles are available under the SIL Open Font Licence: Regular, Italic and Bold. A dependable free option for body text on the web where a classical serif flavour is required without the rendering issues of period originals.
foundry_blurb: Libre Baskerville is a web font optimised for body text at around 16px, based on the American Type Founders' Baskerville from 1941. It features a taller x-height, wider counters and reduced contrast to improve on-screen readability, and is released under the SIL Open Font Licence.

name: Junicode
classification_notes: Classified as serif by the foundry; designed for medieval scholarly texts with broad Unicode coverage including Latin, Runic and Gothic ranges, placing it in the humanist serif tradition suited to historical and literary work.
summary: A specialist serif with over 3,000 characters, built to cover the Medieval Unicode Font Initiative's Latin, Runic and Gothic ranges for scholars of medieval texts.
description: Designed by Peter Baker, Junicode addresses the needs of medievalists requiring broad Unicode coverage across Latin, Runic and Gothic scripts. The family is modest in structure — Regular and Bold, each with an italic — but its character set of over 3,000 glyphs sets it apart from general-purpose serifs. Released under the SIL Open Font License, it is freely available and broadly applicable wherever historical script coverage or period flavour is required.
foundry_blurb: Junicode by Peter Baker is a specialist typeface offering over 3,000 characters in its regular style, with full support for the Medieval Unicode Font Initiative including Latin, Runic and Gothic ranges. It is free under the SIL Open Font License v1.1 and designed for scholars of medieval texts, though usable in any setting requiring rich typographic coverage and historical character.

name: Gap Sans
classification_notes: The foundry classifies it as display; derived from Sani Trixie Sans and reinterpreted in the manner of Kawamata, giving it an unconventional, expressive character suited to display use rather than text.
summary: A three-weight display sans derived from Sani Trixie Sans and reinterpreted through the lens of Kawamata by Alexandre Liziard and Étienne Ozeray.
description: Based on GrandChaos9000's Sani Trixie Sans Regular, Gap Sans was reworked by Alexandre Liziard and Étienne Ozeray to carry a distinctly expressive, Kawamata-influenced character. The family runs across three weights — Regular, Bold and Black — and is released under the SIL Open Font Licence. Its display classification points squarely at headline and poster applications rather than running text.
foundry_blurb: Open Foundry describes Gap Sans Bold as being based on Sani Trixie Sans Regular by GrandChaos9000, reinterpreted in the manner of Kawamata. The typeface was created by Alexandre Liziard and Étienne Ozeray, with a specimen by Michael Seibert, and is available under the SIL Open Font License v.1.1.

name: Inter
classification_notes: Inter is classified as a sans-serif grotesque, designed for screen legibility with a large x-height and open apertures typical of the genre, optimised for digital interfaces rather than print tradition.
summary: A screen-optimised grotesque sans by Rasmus Andersson, covering 147 languages across nine weights with dedicated text and display optical sizes.
description: Designed specifically for digital interfaces, Inter carries a large x-height and open letterforms that maintain legibility at small sizes across screens. The family spans nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a true italic, and includes dedicated optical sizes for text and display use. It has become a de facto standard for UI typography, adopted by GitHub, Mozilla, Figma and Notion, and covers more than 2000 glyphs across 147 languages.
foundry_blurb: Open Foundry hosts Inter as a versatile 21st-century typeface with over 2000 glyphs covering 147 languages. Weights run from Thin 100 to Black 900, with dedicated designs at 100, 400 and 900 to maintain consistent quality. It includes text and display optical sizes and a true italic, and is released under the SIL Open Font License v1.1.

name: Cooper Hewitt
classification_notes: The foundry classifies it as sans-serif; the typeface uses modified-geometric curves and arches, placing it in the geometric sans subcategory despite some humanist inflections in its construction.
summary: A modified-geometric sans designed by Chester Jenkins for the Cooper Hewitt Smithsonian Design Museum, released under the SIL Open Font Licence.
description: Drawn from scratch by Chester Jenkins, originally commissioned by Pentagram for the rebranded Cooper Hewitt Smithsonian Design Museum, the design uses modified-geometric curves rather than strict circular constructions. The family spans eight weights from Thin to Heavy, each with a matching italic, giving it solid utility across institutional and editorial contexts. Letter widths and stroke thicknesses were informed by Jenkins's earlier Polaris Condensed, though the final forms are entirely new.
foundry_blurb: Open Foundry describe Cooper Hewitt as a contemporary sans serif with characters composed of modified-geometric curves and arches. Initially commissioned by Pentagram to evolve Jenkins's Polaris Condensed typeface, Jenkins instead drew everything from scratch, using the existing forms only as a rough guide for letter widths and stroke thicknesses. The font is available under the SIL Open Font License v.1.1.

name: Cotham Sans
classification_notes: The page classifies it directly as sans-serif and describes it as a grotesk, confirming the grotesque subcategory.
summary: A single-weight grotesque sans by Sebastien Sanfilippo, released under the SIL Open Font Licence.
description: A stripped-back grotesque sans with only a Regular weight on offer, making it a narrow choice for projects requiring typographic range. Released under the SIL Open Font Licence, it is freely available for use and modification. The page offers little detail on character set coverage or intended use cases.
foundry_blurb: Open Foundry lists Cotham Sans as a sans-serif grotesk designed by Sebastien Sanfilippo, available in a single Regular weight under the SIL Open Font License v.1.1, with a specimen by Paul Putzar.

name: EB Garamond
classification_notes: An open source revival of Claude Garamont's mid 16th century humanist typeface, based on the Egenolff-Berner specimen of 1592. Humanist serif classification reflects the Renaissance origins and calligraphic quality of the source material.
summary: An open source revival of Claude Garamont's 16th century roman, drawn by Georg Duffner from the 1592 Egenolff-Berner specimen.
description: Georg Duffner's revival takes the 1592 Berner specimen as its primary source, pairing Garamont's roman with Granjon's italic across five weights from Regular to Extrabold. The project name, Egenolff Berner Garamond, acknowledges the specimen's publishers, and the design follows the original letterforms closely rather than interpolating from later interpretations. Available under the SIL Open Font Licence, it is a workable free-to-use option for long-form text where a classical Garamond flavour is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Open Foundry presents EB Garamond Regular as part of an open source revival of Claude Garamont's humanist typeface from the mid 16th century, designed by Georg Duffner. The design is based on the 1592 Berner specimen by Conrad Berner, which displayed Garamont's roman and Granjon's italic in multiple sizes, and from which the project takes its full name: Egenolff Berner Garamond.

name: Bagnard
classification_notes: Classified as serif by the foundry; the irregular, hand-drawn quality inspired by prisoner graffiti suggests a display-oriented serif with expressive, rough characteristics.
summary: A single-weight serif drawn from the graffiti of an anonymous Napoleonic-era prisoner, giving it an irregular, hand-carved quality.
description: Designed by Sebastien Sanfilippo, Bagnard takes its character from inscriptions scratched by a prisoner during the Napoleonic wars, lending the letterforms an uneven, makeshift texture that sets it apart from conventional serifs. Available in a single Regular weight under the SIL Open Font License, it suits display and headline use where a raw, historical tone is appropriate. The specimen was created by Damien Poulain.
foundry_blurb: Open Foundry presents Bagnard as a serif typeface by Sebastien Sanfilippo, inspired by the graffiti of an anonymous Napoleonic-era prisoner, available in a single Regular weight under the SIL Open Font License.
